text: The surgeon begins a laparoscopic myomectomy on a patient with multiple myomas. Three myomas are visible on the anterior uterine wall, with two large and one small. A large myoma is present on the posterior wall's right side. Vasopressin is injected at the base of the anterior myoma, causing a visible color change due to transient ischemia. A myoma screw holds the anterior myoma for traction, while a harmonic scalpel is used to excise the sub-serous myoma, facilitating its separation.

The surgeon deposits the removed anterior myoma into the posterior cul-de-sac. Another anterior subserous myoma is excised and lifted anteriorly using a myoma screw. The suction irrigation instrument is used as a blunt dissector to assist in enucleating the myoma, which is then deposited into the cul-de-sac.

The surgeon addresses the third myoma located on the posterior wall towards the right side. Vasopressin is injected at the base, causing transient ischemia and a change in tissue color to white. The harmonic scalpel cuts the myoma longitudinally to minimize muscle damage. The myoma screw provides traction, while suction irrigation assists in enucleating the soft myoma by separating the capsule in the correct plane. The myoma is successfully removed with minimal bleeding.

Here we are using supraumblycal port that is very effective. If you put the telescope in the umbilicus during the myomectomy, it will be very difficult because it will touch the telescope and you will not get a good field of vision and working space also will be reduced.

The surgeon removes the myoma with minimal muscle injury, avoiding rapid and forceful avulsions. The excess serosa and muscle are left intact to allow natural reorganization. The procedure progresses to the application of tension sutures.
Two small myomas on the posterior wall are visible. The surgeon uses a hook to excise these myomas, cutting with the heel of the hook to facilitate removal.
The surgeon identifies another myoma and begins cutting it longitudinally to open the surface, minimizing bleeding. The myoma is gradually enucleated from the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon uses stone holding forceps to remove a small myoma from the anterior wall near the ostea. The uterus is now free of myomas, and suction and irrigation are performed. Suturing begins with a mattress suture at the base, while all removed myomas are placed on the posterior cul-de-sac for later processing.

The surgeon uses a 35 mm endoskeen needle with number one vicryl suture to obliterate the dead space by stitching through both layers of tissue, ensuring the needle returns back to complete the closure.

The surgeon uses a Clark knot pusher to apply an extracorporeal square knot, approximating the muscle and obliterating the space. The suture is cut long for the assistant's use. The procedure continues with a Dundee jamming knot and intra-corporeal continuous suturing with Aberdeen termination, ensuring the margins are approximated and bleeding is controlled through locking.
The surgeon applies multiple bites in a locking blanket suture, securing the tissue with a surgeon's knot for termination. The suture is tightened, forming a reverse C shape.
The surgeon cuts the suture short and prepares to tie a Western knot, an extracorporeal knot, to secure the remaining structure.
The surgeon feeds the suture through the tissue, pulls it out, and slides it to approximate the tissue edges, completing the suturing process.
The surgeon approximates the serosal layer anteriorly to prevent bleeding, noting the absence of deep intramural involvement in the patient.

The surgeon uses suction irrigation to remove blood from the surgical field. Following this, the process of morcellation begins using an Indian saw-shaped morcellator to gradually morcellate the myomas.

The surgeon uses stone holding forceps to remove broken pieces of the myoma from the surgical field. Following this, a suture passer is employed to close the 15mm port site to prevent incisional hernia.
The surgeon uses vicryl suture to push the suture inside, catches it, pulls it out, and ties the knot, completing the laparoscopic myomectomy procedure.
